quiz_questions.question_text: Salivary amylase begins the chemical digestion of starch, converting it to:

options.text: Amino acids
Maltose (a disaccharide)
Fatty acids and glycerol
Nucleotides

quiz_questions.explanation: Salivary amylase (also called ptyalin) is secreted by the parotid and other salivary glands and begins carbohydrate digestion in the oral cavity by cleaving the alpha-1,4 glycosidic bonds in starch, producing maltose and maltotriose. This enzyme is inactivated when it reaches the acidic environment of the stomach.

quiz_questions.question_text: Pepsin digests proteins in the:

options.text: Mouth, where it is activated by saliva
Stomach, where the acidic pH (1.5-3.5) activates pepsinogen and provides an optimal environment
Duodenum, where it works alongside trypsin
Large intestine, where it breaks down undigested proteins

quiz_questions.explanation: Pepsin is the primary gastric protease, formed when hydrochloric acid from parietal cells cleaves the inactive zymogen pepsinogen (secreted by chief cells) into its active form. Pepsin functions optimally at pH 1.5-3.5 and cleaves proteins into smaller polypeptides, initiating protein digestion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Pancreatic juice enters the duodenum through the:

options.text: Pyloric sphincter
Hepatopancreatic ampulla (ampulla of Vater)
Ileocecal valve
Common hepatic duct

quiz_questions.explanation: The main pancreatic duct (duct of Wirsung) joins the common bile duct at the hepatopancreatic ampulla (ampulla of Vater) in the wall of the duodenum. The sphincter of Oddi regulates the flow of both pancreatic juice and bile into the duodenal lumen.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which hormone stimulates parietal cells to produce hydrochloric acid?

options.text: Secretin
CCK
Gastrin
GIP (gastric inhibitory peptide)

quiz_questions.explanation: Gastrin is released by G-cells in the gastric antrum in response to stomach distension, peptides in the lumen, and vagal stimulation. It acts on parietal cells to stimulate HCl secretion via the CCK-B receptor and also promotes gastric motility and mucosal growth.

quiz_questions.question_text: CCK (cholecystokinin) is released in response to fat and protein in the duodenum and stimulates:

options.text: Gastric acid secretion by parietal cells
Gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion
Salivary gland secretion of amylase
Water absorption in the large intestine

quiz_questions.explanation: CCK is secreted by I-cells in the duodenal and jejunal mucosa when fatty acids and amino acids are detected. It causes the gallbladder to contract and release concentrated bile for fat emulsification, stimulates pancreatic acinar cells to secrete digestive enzymes, and also inhibits gastric emptying to allow adequate duodenal processing time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Secretin stimulates the pancreas to secrete:

options.text: Digestive enzymes (lipase, proteases, amylase)
Bicarbonate-rich fluid that neutralizes acidic chyme in the duodenum
Insulin to lower blood glucose
Bile salts for fat emulsification

quiz_questions.explanation: Secretin is released by S-cells in the duodenal mucosa when acidic chyme (pH < 4.5) enters from the stomach. It stimulates the pancreatic duct cells to secrete a watery, bicarbonate-rich fluid that neutralizes the acid, raising the duodenal pH to approximately 7-8, which is essential for optimal pancreatic enzyme activity.

quiz_questions.question_text: GIP (gastric inhibitory peptide/glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide) functions to:

options.text: Increase gastric acid secretion and motility
Inhibit gastric acid secretion and motility while stimulating insulin release from pancreatic beta cells
Stimulate bile production by the liver
Increase peristalsis throughout the entire GI tract

quiz_questions.explanation: GIP is released by K-cells in the duodenum and jejunum in response to glucose and fat. It has a dual role: it inhibits gastric acid secretion and motility (slowing gastric emptying) and acts as an incretin hormone that stimulates insulin release from pancreatic beta cells in anticipation of nutrient absorption, helping to maintain glucose homeostasis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Glucose is absorbed across the apical membrane of enterocytes via:

options.text: Simple diffusion down its concentration gradient
Sodium-dependent co-transport (SGLT1), then exits via GLUT2 on the basolateral membrane
Osmosis through aquaporin water channels
Receptor-mediated endocytosis

quiz_questions.explanation: Glucose absorption in the small intestine is a two-step process: SGLT1 (sodium-glucose linked transporter 1) on the apical brush border actively co-transports glucose and sodium into the enterocyte against glucose's concentration gradient. Glucose then exits the cell on the basolateral side via GLUT2 facilitated diffusion into the blood capillaries.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fatty acids are absorbed into enterocytes by:

options.text: Active transport using ATP-dependent pumps
Simple diffusion across the lipid bilayer (since they are lipid-soluble), then repackaged into chylomicrons and released into lacteals
Sodium-dependent co-transport similar to glucose
Receptor-mediated endocytosis of micelles

quiz_questions.explanation: After bile salt emulsification and lipase digestion, free fatty acids and monoglycerides are carried in micelles to the enterocyte brush border. Being lipid-soluble, they diffuse directly across the phospholipid bilayer of the cell membrane. Inside the enterocyte, they are re-esterified to triglycerides, packaged with cholesterol and proteins into chylomicrons, and secreted into lacteals.

quiz_questions.question_text: The cephalic phase of gastric secretion is triggered by:

options.text: Food physically entering and distending the stomach
The sight, smell, thought, or taste of food, mediated by the vagus nerve before food reaches the stomach
Chyme entering the duodenum and triggering hormonal feedback
Distension of the large intestine activating the defecation reflex

quiz_questions.explanation: The cephalic phase accounts for approximately 30% of total gastric secretion and occurs before food reaches the stomach. Sensory input (sight, smell, taste, or even thought of food) stimulates the cerebral cortex and hypothalamus, sending signals via the vagus nerve (cranial nerve X) to activate parietal cells, chief cells, and G-cells in the stomach.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is glycogenesis?

options.text: The breakdown of glycogen into glucose to raise blood sugar levels
The synthesis of glycogen from excess glucose in the liver and skeletal muscle for energy storage, stimulated by insulin
The creation of new glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors during fasting
The conversion of amino acids directly into glycogen

quiz_questions.explanation: Glycogenesis is the metabolic pathway by which glucose molecules are linked together by glycogen synthase to form glycogen, a highly branched polysaccharide stored primarily in the liver (to maintain blood glucose) and skeletal muscle (for local energy needs). This process is stimulated by insulin after a carbohydrate-rich meal when blood glucose levels are elevated.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is gluconeogenesis?

options.text: The synthesis of glycogen from glucose after a meal
The metabolic pathway by which the liver synthesizes new glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors (amino acids, lactate, glycerol) during fasting to maintain blood glucose levels
The breakdown of stored fat into free fatty acids for energy
The fermentation of glucose by gut bacteria in the colon

quiz_questions.explanation: Gluconeogenesis occurs primarily in the liver (and to a lesser extent in the kidneys) during fasting or starvation when glycogen stores are depleted. The liver converts non-carbohydrate substrates such as amino acids (especially alanine), lactate (from anaerobic glycolysis), and glycerol (from fat breakdown) into glucose to maintain blood glucose levels for glucose-dependent tissues like the brain and red blood cells.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the gastric phase of digestion?

options.text: The phase of digestion that occurs before food enters the mouth, triggered by anticipation
The phase of gastric secretion triggered when food enters and distends the stomach, causing local reflexes and gastrin release that stimulate HCl and pepsinogen secretion
The phase that begins when chyme enters the duodenum and inhibits further gastric activity
The phase of digestion that occurs exclusively in the large intestine

quiz_questions.explanation: The gastric phase accounts for approximately 60% of total gastric secretion and is initiated by stomach distension (activating stretch receptors), chemical stimulation by peptides and amino acids (activating chemoreceptors), and local reflexes. These stimuli cause G-cells to release gastrin, which stimulates parietal cells to produce HCl and chief cells to secrete pepsinogen.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the intestinal phase of digestion?

options.text: The phase in which the stomach increases acid secretion to maximum levels

The phase triggered when chyme enters the duodenum, which primarily inhibits gastric secretion and motility through hormonal (secretin, CCK, GIP) and neural reflexes to prevent overwhelming the small intestine
The phase of digestion that occurs exclusively in the mouth during mastication
A phase of digestion that only activates the large intestine

quiz_questions.explanation: The intestinal phase begins when chyme enters the duodenum and primarily functions to slow gastric emptying and secretion so the small intestine is not overwhelmed. Acid triggers secretin release (pancreatic bicarbonate), fats and proteins trigger CCK release (bile and enzymes), and GIP inhibits gastric activity. These mechanisms coordinate the rate of gastric emptying with the duodenum's processing capacity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'glycogenolysis' mean?

options.text: The synthesis of new glucose from amino acids and lactate in the liver
The breakdown of stored glycogen into glucose, stimulated by glucagon and epinephrine, to raise blood glucose levels during fasting or stress
The conversion of glucose to glycogen for storage after a meal
The breakdown of fats into fatty acids and glycerol

quiz_questions.explanation: Glycogenolysis is the catabolic process by which glycogen phosphorylase cleaves glucose units from stored glycogen. In the liver, this releases glucose into the blood to maintain blood glucose levels between meals. In muscle, the glucose is used locally for energy. Glucagon (from pancreatic alpha cells) and epinephrine (from the adrenal medulla) are the primary hormonal stimulators of glycogenolysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'enterohepatic circulation' mean?

options.text: The flow of arterial blood from the hepatic artery to the liver sinusoids
The recycling pathway by which bile salts are secreted into the duodenum, reabsorbed in the terminal ileum, and returned to the liver via the portal vein for re-secretion in bile
The pathway by which nutrients travel from the intestine to the liver
The lymphatic drainage of the intestine into the thoracic duct

quiz_questions.explanation: The enterohepatic circulation conserves the body's bile salt pool by recycling approximately 95% of bile salts secreted into the duodenum. Active transport in the terminal ileum returns bile salts to the portal circulation, delivering them to hepatocytes for re-secretion in bile. This cycle occurs 6-8 times daily and is essential for maintaining adequate bile salt concentrations for fat digestion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'incretin effect' mean?

options.text: The increase in gastric acid secretion that occurs after a large meal

The phenomenon in which oral glucose consumption stimulates significantly more insulin release than the same amount of glucose given intravenously, due to GI hormones (GIP and GLP-1) that potentiate insulin secretion
The gradual increase in enzyme production by the pancreas during growth
The increased absorption rate that occurs after a period of fasting

quiz_questions.explanation: The incretin effect explains why oral glucose produces a greater insulin response than IV glucose: GI hormones called incretins (GIP from K-cells and GLP-1 from L-cells) are released in response to nutrients in the gut lumen and act on pancreatic beta cells to enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion. This is the basis for incretin-based diabetes medications (GLP-1 receptor agonists and DPP-4 inhibitors).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the vagus nerve's role in digestive physiology?

options.text: It only carries pain signals from the GI tract to the brain

As the primary parasympathetic nerve to the GI tract, it stimulates gastric secretion, increases motility, promotes pancreatic output, and relaxes sphincters to facilitate digestion ('rest and digest')
It inhibits all digestive processes as part of the sympathetic nervous system
It controls only the voluntary act of swallowing and has no effect on the stomach or intestines

quiz_questions.explanation: The vagus nerve (cranial nerve X) is the main parasympathetic nerve supply to the GI tract from the esophagus to the splenic flexure. It stimulates parietal cells (HCl), chief cells (pepsinogen), G-cells (gastrin), pancreatic secretion, gallbladder contraction, and intestinal motility. Vagotomy (surgical cutting of the vagus nerve) was historically used to treat peptic ulcers by reducing acid secretion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'stercobilin' refer to?

options.text: A bile salt produced by the liver for fat emulsification
The brown pigment produced by bacterial metabolism of bilirubin in the colon that gives feces their characteristic color
A digestive enzyme that breaks down cellulose in plant fiber
A hormone that stimulates defecation

quiz_questions.explanation: When conjugated bilirubin reaches the colon via bile, colonic bacteria convert it to urobilinogen. Most urobilinogen is further converted to stercobilin, the brown pigment responsible for the normal color of feces. Some urobilinogen is reabsorbed and excreted by the kidneys as urobilin, which gives urine its yellow color. Absence of stercobilin (in bile duct obstruction) causes pale, acholic stools.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'short bowel syndrome' mean?

options.text: A congenital condition in which the intestine is shorter than normal at birth but functions normally
A malabsorptive condition resulting from insufficient functional small intestinal length (usually after surgical resection), leading to inadequate absorption of nutrients, fluids, and electrolytes
A condition in which the large intestine is too short to absorb adequate water
Temporary malabsorption after a gastrointestinal infection that resolves within weeks

quiz_questions.explanation: Short bowel syndrome occurs when the remaining functional small intestine (normally 300-600 cm in adults) is insufficient to maintain adequate nutrition and hydration through enteral absorption alone. It most commonly results from extensive surgical resection for Crohn's disease, mesenteric ischemia, or radiation enteritis. Management may include dietary modifications, medications to slow transit, and in severe cases, long-term TPN.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student studying for the AP exam must trace the complete digestion of a ham and cheese sandwich. As the sandwich enters the mouth, which specific digestive processes begin immediately?

options.text: Protein digestion by pepsin and fat digestion by lipase begin immediately
Mechanical digestion via mastication (teeth grinding food) and chemical digestion of starch by salivary amylase begin, while lingual lipase starts minor fat digestion
Only mechanical digestion occurs; no chemical digestion happens until the stomach
All three macronutrients (protein, carbs, fat) are fully digested in the mouth

quiz_questions.explanation: In the mouth, mastication physically breaks the sandwich into smaller pieces while the tongue mixes it with saliva. Salivary amylase immediately begins hydrolyzing starch (from the bread) into maltose. Lingual lipase, secreted by von Ebner's glands at the back of the tongue, begins minor fat digestion. Protein digestion does not begin until the stomach, where pepsin is activated by HCl.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physiology instructor asks students to explain what happens hormonally when acidic chyme first enters the duodenum from the stomach. A student correctly describes the following sequence of events:

options.text: Gastrin is released to increase stomach acid production, accelerating gastric emptying

Secretin is released by S-cells in response to acid (stimulating pancreatic bicarbonate to neutralize the acid), while CCK is released by I-cells in response to fat and protein (stimulating gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion)
Only GIP is released, which stimulates insulin and has no effect on the pancreas or gallbladder
The vagus nerve is inhibited, completely stopping all gastric and intestinal activity

quiz_questions.explanation: When acidic chyme enters the duodenum, two key hormones are released: secretin (triggered by low pH) stimulates pancreatic duct cells to release bicarbonate-rich fluid that neutralizes the acid, while CCK (triggered by fats and amino acids) stimulates gallbladder contraction and pancreatic acinar cell enzyme secretion. Together, these hormones coordinate the duodenal response to incoming chyme.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with type 2 diabetes is prescribed a GLP-1 receptor agonist medication. The physician explains that this drug mimics a natural GI hormone. How does understanding the incretin effect help explain the mechanism of this diabetes medication?

options.text: The drug replaces insulin that the pancreas can no longer produce
The drug mimics GLP-1, an incretin hormone released by intestinal L-cells that enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppresses glucagon, slows gastric emptying, and promotes satiety
The drug blocks glucose absorption in the small intestine, preventing blood sugar spikes
The drug stimulates the liver to produce more glycogen, removing glucose from the blood

quiz_questions.explanation: GLP-1 (glucagon-like peptide-1) is an incretin hormone released by L-cells in the ileum and colon after eating. It enhances insulin secretion (glucose-dependent, reducing hypoglycemia risk), suppresses glucagon secretion, slows gastric emptying (reducing postprandial glucose spikes), and acts on the brain to promote satiety. GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) exploit these effects to treat type 2 diabetes and obesity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is asked on an exam to explain why a patient who undergoes a vagotomy (surgical cutting of the vagus nerve) experiences decreased gastric acid secretion. What is the correct explanation?

options.text: The vagus nerve directly produces hydrochloric acid, so cutting it stops acid production entirely

The vagus nerve provides parasympathetic stimulation that directly activates parietal cells to produce HCl and stimulates G-cells to release gastrin; severing it eliminates both the neural and humoral stimulation of acid secretion
The vagus nerve carries sensory signals from the stomach; without these signals, parietal cells cannot sense food and stop producing acid
Vagotomy causes the stomach to produce more mucus, which dilutes the acid

quiz_questions.explanation: The vagus nerve stimulates gastric acid secretion through two mechanisms: (1) direct stimulation of parietal cells via acetylcholine acting on muscarinic M3 receptors, and (2) stimulation of G-cells to release gastrin, which further drives acid production. Vagotomy eliminates both pathways, reducing both the cephalic phase and the neural component of the gastric phase of acid secretion.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with liver cirrhosis is found to have a fasting blood glucose of 55 mg/dL (hypoglycemic). His doctor explains that the liver's impaired ability to perform certain metabolic functions is responsible. Which hepatic processes are failing?

options.text: The liver can no longer produce insulin to regulate blood glucose

The cirrhotic liver has impaired glycogenolysis (breaking down glycogen stores) and gluconeogenesis (synthesizing new glucose from non-carbohydrate sources), both of which are essential for maintaining blood glucose during fasting
The liver can no longer absorb glucose from the portal vein
The damaged liver overproduces glucagon, which paradoxically lowers blood sugar

quiz_questions.explanation: The liver is the central organ for blood glucose homeostasis during fasting. It maintains blood glucose through glycogenolysis (releasing stored glucose from glycogen) in the early fasting state and gluconeogenesis (creating new glucose from amino acids, lactate, and glycerol) during prolonged fasting. In severe cirrhosis, the loss of functional hepatocytes impairs both processes, leading to fasting hypoglycemia.

quiz_questions.question_text: An AP exam question asks students to compare how glucose versus fatty acid absorption differs at the cellular level in the small intestine. A student provides the following correct comparison:

options.text: Both are absorbed by simple diffusion since they are both small molecules

Glucose requires active co-transport with sodium via SGLT1 (energy-dependent), while fatty acids diffuse passively across the lipid membrane (no energy required) after being delivered to the brush border in micelles
Both require sodium-dependent active transport across the apical membrane
Glucose enters via lacteals while fatty acids enter via blood capillaries

quiz_questions.explanation: This comparison illustrates a key concept in digestive physiology: glucose, being water-soluble, cannot cross the lipid bilayer passively and requires the SGLT1 transporter (secondary active transport using the sodium gradient). Fatty acids, being lipid-soluble, can diffuse directly across the phospholipid bilayer of the enterocyte membrane once micelles deliver them to the brush border surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physiology professor presents a case of a patient whose stool has turned pale/clay-colored. She asks students to trace the pathway of bilirubin and explain why bile duct obstruction would cause this finding.

options.text: Bile duct obstruction prevents bile salts from reaching the colon, and bile salts are what give stool its color

Bile duct obstruction prevents conjugated bilirubin from reaching the intestine, where it would normally be converted by bacteria to stercobilin (the brown pigment of feces), resulting in pale, acholic stools
The obstruction causes the liver to stop producing bilirubin entirely
The blocked bile causes excessive fat in the stool, which is white and masks the normal color

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal pathway is: hemoglobin is broken down to bilirubin, which is conjugated in the liver and excreted in bile into the duodenum. In the colon, bacteria convert bilirubin to urobilinogen and then to stercobilin, which gives feces their brown color. When the bile duct is obstructed, bilirubin cannot reach the intestine, so stercobilin is not produced, resulting in pale (acholic) stools.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient who had extensive ileal resection for Crohn's disease develops both fat malabsorption (steatorrhea) and vitamin B12 deficiency. A student is asked to explain how losing the terminal ileum causes both problems through different mechanisms.

options.text: The ileum produces lipase and intrinsic factor, so removing it eliminates both

Loss of the terminal ileum disrupts enterohepatic bile salt recycling (depleting the bile salt pool needed for fat emulsification, causing steatorrhea) and eliminates the specific site where the intrinsic factor-B12 complex is absorbed
The ileum normally absorbs all dietary fat directly, so its removal causes fat to pass through unabsorbed
The ileum produces CCK and secretin, so its loss prevents gallbladder and pancreatic function

quiz_questions.explanation: The terminal ileum serves two critical and distinct functions: (1) it is the site of active bile salt reabsorption via the enterohepatic circulation (losing it depletes the bile salt pool faster than the liver can compensate, impairing fat emulsification and causing steatorrhea), and (2) it contains the specific receptors (cubilin) that bind the intrinsic factor-B12 complex for absorption. These are independent mechanisms that both fail when the ileum is resected.

quiz_questions.question_text: During an AP exam review session, a student is asked to explain why sympathetic nervous system activation ('fight or flight') inhibits digestion while parasympathetic activation ('rest and digest') promotes it. What is the physiological rationale?

options.text: The sympathetic and parasympathetic systems have identical effects on digestion but operate at different times of day

During 'fight or flight,' sympathetic activation diverts blood flow from the GI tract to skeletal muscles and inhibits secretion and motility, because digestion is not a survival priority. During 'rest and digest,' parasympathetic (vagal) activation increases secretion, motility, and blood flow to promote efficient digestion
The sympathetic system destroys digestive enzymes while the parasympathetic system produces them
Both systems promote digestion equally, but the sympathetic system also adds adrenaline to speed up the process

quiz_questions.explanation: This reflects the body's resource allocation strategy. During stress, the sympathetic system releases norepinephrine that constricts splanchnic blood vessels (redirecting blood to muscles), inhibits GI motility and secretion, and contracts sphincters. The parasympathetic system (via the vagus nerve and acetylcholine) does the opposite: it increases blood flow to the gut, stimulates secretion of HCl, enzymes, and bile, enhances motility, and relaxes sphincters to facilitate digestion.

quiz_questions.question_text: An AP exam essay question asks: 'A patient eats a meal containing starch, protein, and fat. Trace the complete hormonal regulation of digestion from the cephalic phase through the intestinal phase, identifying the key hormones and their effects.' Which response demonstrates the most complete understanding?

options.text: Gastrin is the only hormone involved, stimulating all phases of digestion from mouth to colon

Cephalic phase: vagal stimulation prepares the stomach. Gastric phase: food distension triggers gastrin release (HCl, motility). Intestinal phase: acid triggers secretin (pancreatic bicarbonate), fat/protein triggers CCK (gallbladder contraction, pancreatic enzymes), and GIP inhibits gastric activity while stimulating insulin for glucose uptake
CCK controls the cephalic phase, secretin controls the gastric phase, and gastrin controls the intestinal phase
The cephalic and gastric phases have no hormonal involvement; only the intestinal phase uses hormones

quiz_questions.explanation: This comprehensive answer traces the full regulatory cascade: the cephalic phase (vagus nerve prepares the stomach before food arrives), the gastric phase (gastrin from G-cells drives HCl and pepsinogen secretion), and the intestinal phase (secretin neutralizes acid via bicarbonate, CCK triggers bile release and enzyme secretion for fat and protein digestion, and GIP provides negative feedback on the stomach while linking digestion to metabolic glucose control via insulin). This integration of neural and hormonal control exemplifies the systems-level thinking required for AP-level understanding.
